structuredFacts.title: Python (programming language)
structuredFacts.subtitle: General-purpose programming language
structuredFacts.leadParagraph:
Python is a high-level, general-purpose programming language that emphasizes code readability, simplicity, and ease-of-writing with the use of significant indentation, an extensive ("batteries-included") standard library, and garbage collection. Python supports multiple programming paradigms but with an emphasis on object-oriented programming and dynamic typing.
Guido van Rossum began working on Python in the late 1980s as a successor to the ABC programming language. Python 3.0, released in 2008, was a major revision and not completely backward-compatible with earlier versions. Beginning with Python 3.5, capabilities and keywords for typing were added to the language, allowing optional static typing. As of 2026, the Python Software Foundation supports Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, 3.13, and 3.14, following the project's annual release cycle and five-year support policy. Python 3.15 is currently in the beta development phase, and the stable release is expected to launch in October 2026. Earlier versions in the 3.x series have reached end-of-life and no longer receive security updates. 
Python is widely taught as an introductory programming language.
structuredFacts.briefSummary: Conceived by Guido van Rossum at CWI in the Netherlands during the late 1980s, Python officially began development in 1989. Van Rossum designed it to succeed the ABC programming language, drawing its name from the British comedy series Monty Python's Flying Circus. He guided the community as its Benevolent Dictator for Life until he stepped down in 2018. To govern the language after his departure, the community elected a five-member Steering Council in 2019. Today, Python stands as a high-level, general-purpose language that prioritizes simplicity, readability, and significant indentation.

cards.title: The Birth of Python
cards.summary: Conceived by Guido van Rossum in the late 1980s, Python was born at CWI in the Netherlands. His goal was to design a successor to the ABC programming language that could overcome its inherent limitations. Official development of the language began in 1989. This debut established a new programming tool that prioritized code readability over sheer execution speed. Indeed, Python was crafted specifically to succeed the ABC language. Ultimately, this new language emphasized clear readability to improve upon the shortcomings of its predecessor.
cards.readerQuestion: What drove the creation of a new programming language in the late 1980s?
cards.keyTakeaway: Python succeeded ABC by prioritizing code readability over execution speed.

cards.title: The Architect of Python
cards.summary: In 1989, Guido van Rossum designed Python's core architecture using dynamic typing, late binding, and reference-counting garbage collection . He wanted a highly readable, modular successor to the ABC language that prioritized developer simplicity . Van Rossum chose the name to honor the British comedy series Monty Python's Flying Circus . This playful branding fostered a unique, welcoming culture that propelled Python to global dominance . Van Rossum guided this community as its 'Benevolent Dictator for Life' until his resignation in 2018 . Ultimately, his dual focus on clean code and community governance shaped Python's enduring legacy .
cards.readerQuestion: How did Guido van Rossum design Python's core architecture and unique culture?
cards.keyTakeaway: Guido van Rossum's design choices shaped Python's enduring architecture.

provenance.fact: Python established a unique culture and a robust architectural foundation that sustained its growth into one of the world's most popular programming languages.

provenance.sentence: Ultimately, his dual focus on clean code and community governance shaped Python's enduring legacy .
provenance.fact: Guido van Rossum's design choices shaped Python's enduring architecture and culture.

cards.title: The Evolution of Python
cards.summary: Supporting object-oriented, structured, functional, and aspect-oriented paradigms, Python is inherently versatile. Guido van Rossum designed this extensible architecture specifically to improve upon the ABC language. At the heart of this design lies a core philosophy summarized in the 'Zen of Python'. By prioritizing clarity and readability, this approach drove widespread global adoption across both software development and education. Even standard tutorials pay homage to this history, using 'spam' and 'eggs' as a tribute to Monty Python. Ultimately, it was this combination of flexible paradigm support and a clear philosophy that secured Python's global dominance.
cards.readerQuestion: How did Python evolve to support diverse programming paradigms and gain widespread adoption?
cards.keyTakeaway: Flexible paradigms and clear philosophy drove Python's global adoption.

cards.title: The Great Version Divide
cards.summary: Python emerged as a high-level, general-purpose language that prioritized readability, simplicity, and significant indentation. This design philosophy faced a massive shift when Python 3.0 introduced backward-incompatible syntax and semantics. Earlier, in 2000, Python 2.0 had debuted with list comprehensions and garbage collection. Consequently, the community endured a prolonged transition until Python 2 officially retired in 2020. Python 3.0 arrived in 2008 as a major, backward-incompatible revision with changed syntax and new semantics. Ultimately, this painful backward incompatibility split the community but successfully modernized the language.
cards.readerQuestion: How did the transition from Python 2 to Python 3 reshape the community?
cards.keyTakeaway: Backward incompatibility split the community but ultimately modernized the language.

cards.title: Python After the Dictator
cards.summary: When Guido van Rossum stepped down as BDFL in 2018, it led to the election of a five-member Steering Council in 2019 to govern the language. His resignation as Benevolent Dictator for Life followed intense conflicts over assignment expressions. Interestingly, in Python culture, describing a piece of code or a solution as 'clever' is actually not considered a compliment. To guide its multi-paradigm architecture, Python transitioned to a decentralized governance model led by this elected five-member Steering Council. The language maintains a multi-paradigm architecture, primarily supporting object-oriented programming, dynamic typing, and structured programming. Today, Python thrives under collective governance while preserving its core philosophy.
cards.readerQuestion: How does Python govern itself and maintain its philosophy in the post-BDFL era?
cards.keyTakeaway: Python thrives under collective governance while preserving its core philosophy.

namedEntities.name: Guido van Rossum
namedEntities.description: Dutch programmer who created the Python programming language and served as its chief decision-maker until 2018.

namedEntities.name: Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica
namedEntities.description: The national research institute for mathematics and computer science in the Netherlands where Python was conceived.

namedEntities.name: Python Software Foundation
namedEntities.description: The non-profit organization that manages and supports the Python programming language.

namedEntities.name: ABC
namedEntities.description: An imperative general-purpose programming language and environment developed at CWI that served as Python's predecessor.

namedEntities.name: Monty Python
namedEntities.description: A British surreal comedy group whose television show inspired the name of the Python programming language.

namedEntities.name: Tim Peters
namedEntities.description: A software developer and major contributor to the Python community who wrote the Zen of Python.

namedEntities.name: PyPy
namedEntities.description: An alternative, highly compatible implementation of the Python programming language that features a just-in-time compiler.

timeline.headline: Python Conceived by Van Rossum
timeline.description: Guido van Rossum conceived Python at CWI in the Netherlands as a successor to the ABC programming language.

timeline.headline: First Public Release 0.9.0
timeline.description: Guido van Rossum released Python version 0.9.0, featuring exception handling and Amoeba operating system interfacing.

timeline.headline: Python 2.0 Released
timeline.description: Python 2.0 introduced list comprehensions, cycle-detecting garbage collection, reference counting, and Unicode support.

timeline.headline: Python 3.0 Major Revision
timeline.description: Python 3.0 was released as a major, backward-incompatible revision with changed syntax and new semantics.

timeline.headline: Van Rossum Steps Down
timeline.description: Guido van Rossum resigned from his role as Benevolent Dictator for Life following conflicts over assignment expressions.

timeline.headline: Steering Council Formed
timeline.description: Active Python core developers elected a five-member Steering Council to lead the project's governance.

timeline.headline: Python 2 End of Life
timeline.description: Python 2.7.18 was released as the final version, officially ending support and security updates for Python 2.
